*Includes people in prison for new offenses and with sentence lengths that are not less than one year or life sentences.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In Maryland, people who are convicted of nonviolent crimes with sentences of more than six months become eligible for parole after serving one-fourth of the maximum sentence. People in prison convicted of a “violent crime” become eligible for parole after serving one-half of the maximum.¹
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility In 2020, 55 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 2 percent. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 64 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 97 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 32 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 79 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 19 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for murder and non-negligent manslaughter (27%) and aggravated or simple assault (19%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, 30 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## State Notes Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Porgram (NCRP) for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY is treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000.

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows:First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on thepercentage of the max. sentence (25 percentand ). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in thereport or previous reports, it is used as the best estimate. Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent are missing PEY (relevant) cases exclude those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available.

Estimates for the percent of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percent of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percent of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percent of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's departmet of corrections' report (2023).⁴ # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 33 percent, changing from 22,645 in 2010 to 15,134 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2022, 71 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2022, 96 percent of people in prison were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2022, 34 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 25 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 53 percent, dropping from 12,866 in 2010 to 6,048 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 37 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 7 percent increase compared to 2010. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 79 percent of prison releases were conditional releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 70 percent of prison releases were Black, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 93 percent of prison releases were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 39 percent of prison releases were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 19 percent of prison releases were for people incarcerated for drugs offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ### Gender Gender disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
:::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, Black people spent 0.3 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 0.6 more years on average in prison compared to White people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, where Black people spent 5.8 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender In 2020, females spent 0.9 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Maryland. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by gender in 2020. For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 3.1 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Disparities in Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pe_rri_infographic_black_Maryland.png){width=1188} ::: ::: In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 5.8 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Maryland. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_Maryland.png){width=1172} ::: ::: In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Maryland. ### Gender :::
## Years in Prison After Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Black people spent an average of 0.2 more years in prison past parole eligibility and Hispanic people spent an average of 0.2 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people.

``` ::: {.cell} ::: This chart shows the average years in prison past parole eligibility for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity observed was for murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, where people of Black, non-Hispanic had 3.1 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to Hispanic people people, who had the shortest time in prison past parole eligibility for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` This chart shows the average years in prison past parole eligibility for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 2.5 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.* :::
## End Notes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Maryland *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, December 2020), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/maryland_doi_report_3_3_21.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/maryland_doi_report_3_3_21.pdf).

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., "American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report" (Minneapolis, Minnesoda: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, pp. 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see [pending].

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.[pending] Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program).
